posts.text:
Amid a backdrop of war, this Sunday’s Oscars will celebrate some of the greatest art of the past year. Among those in contention: the Iranian filmmakers behind the documentary “Cutting Through Rocks”, who tell me they’re hopeful for change.

Video

From the archives, a look inside the religious fervor that fuels Iran’s military. In 1985, CNN got an exclusive look at the Basij and how children were used as human minesweepers on the country’s frontlines during the Iran-Iraq war.

Video

This week, Iran and Hezbollah launched their first joint attacks on Israel, while Iran has been using cluster munitions to try and pierce Israeli defences. @JDiamond1 saw the destruction on the ground, in this report.

Video
Back in 2015, I spoke with John about his incredible reporting, from Bosnia to Afghanistan to Iraq. You can watch that conversation here: edition.cnn.com/videos/world…

A true loss to journalism and a brilliant career. He was one of the frontline journo-warriors in Sarajevo. RIP.


The New York Times (@nytimes)

Breaking News: John F. Burns, a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist for The New York Times best known for his work in Iraq, died at 81. nyti.ms/4lp2qq6




— https://nitter.net/nytimes/status/2032484958208422066#m

“The wish fulfilment fantasy that somehow American bombs are going to result in homegrown democracy in the region has to be put to bed,” says Iranian-American scholar Reza Aslan. Instead, he sees “a consolidation of hardline power” in Iran, as US-Israeli bombing continues.

Video

Fmr. Trump official Elliott Abrams: “I find it hard to understand why [the Strait of Hormuz wasn’t] factored in.” Were he advising Trump now, he’d tell him, “worry less… about gas prices,” and more about degrading Iran’s military & nuclear capabilities until the end of his term.

Video

What is President Trump’s Iran exit strategy? His fmr. envoy Elliott Abrams says “either there is some kind of an uprising against the regime or in probably a week or two, the president will call it off” and say they’ve hit all they need to. The latter, he thinks, is more likely.

Video

Ali Larijani, Iran’s top national security official who is running the country, outside on a pro-government demo, despite drones and assassinations from the sky, saying Trump has underestimated the Iranian people.


Ragıp Soylu (@ragipsoylu)

Top Iranian national security official Ali Larijani keeps marching after Israeli strikes in Tehran near the area. 

He even gives a live interview. 

“Trump's problem is that he doesn't realize that the Iranian nation is mature and determined”

Video

"We're looking at a deployment of the Venezuelan model [in Cuba]," says Jeffrey DeLaurentis, who was America's first ambassador to Cuba in 55 years. "I don't really like the notion of using humanitarian suffering as a political tool for change. But this is where we are."

Video

"In Lebanon they believe that the U.S. is giving Israel much longer leash," @ronenbergman tells me. "This could take very long, much longer unless the U.S. orders Israel to stop. This could just continue [as] some kind of a war of attrition."

Video

"I think we are quite certain that he was injured. And now he's being taken care of in some kind of a medical facility." @ronenbergman on speculation about Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei who has not yet been seen publicly since his appointment as Supreme Leader

Video

Are the U.S. and Israel still aligned on their war in Iran? @ronenbergman tells me "in spite of all the predictions that at a certain point Trump will just be tired or pissed off with Netanyahu, they continue to be aligned... but now it is an American order for the Israelis not to strike anything from critical infrastructure, to storage of oil or gas without getting a pre-permission from the U.S."

Video

.@MaryamAlemzadeh is an expert on the IRGC and tells me that while the Islamic Republic has been militarily weakened, “that’s never been the strength of the IRGC,” arguing its penetration is still intact: 

“It's not going to be an easy takeover for the protesters, who I believe are the majority of the population now, because the weapons are still in the hands of the IRGC and Basij, and they do not need the more advanced equipment that has been destroyed to some extent in order to repress a popu
